title: St. Arsenius (Monthly) — St. Glyceria the Martyr & St. Alexander of Tiberias
narrative:
St. Arsenius the Great Desert Father, St. Glyceria the Virgin-Martyr, and St. Alexander the Charcoal-Seller of Tiberias

On the thirteenth of Ginbot, the holy Church celebrates the monthly commemoration of the holy, venerable, and God-bearing Arsenius the Great, the Roman tutor who became the most silent of the desert fathers — a man whose entire spiritual method can be summarised in the three maxims the tradition attributes to him: flee from men, be silent, and rest in stillness. Arsenius is commemorated monthly because his teaching represents one of the permanent poles of Christian spirituality: the pole of withdrawal, of the desert, of the reduction of noise and word and social obligation to the minimum that allows the voice of God to be heard. In a world that grows louder with each generation, the monthly feast of Arsenius is the Church's recurring invitation to the community to remember that silence is not the absence of speech but the presence of God — that the God who spoke creation into being does not always speak in the audible frequencies.

The spiritual teaching of Arsenius, as preserved in the Apophthegmata Patrum, centres on a paradox that has fascinated the tradition: this man who had been educated at the highest levels of Roman culture, who had served as tutor to the sons of the emperor, and who therefore had more to contribute to theological conversation than almost any other monk in Egypt, is the one the tradition remembers for saying nothing. His silence was not ignorance but fullness: the silence of a vessel so filled that it cannot be poured out without loss. The tradition understands Arsenius's silence as the practical consequence of taking seriously the Johannine principle that the Word encompasses all things — that if everything is contained in the Word, then any human word that is not directly necessary is, at best, redundant and, at worst, a distraction from the Word itself. His monthly feast is the Church's periodic reminder that the most important things cannot be said.

Saint Glyceria the Virgin-Martyr is one of the most beloved of the early martyrs, a young woman of Rome who was living in Heraclea in Thrace when the Antonine persecution reached her city. She is remembered for a particular act of witness that has made her iconic in the tradition: she was brought to the temple of Zeus and ordered to offer incense, and instead she took the incense and hurled it at the idol, then turned to the assembled crowd and cried out a confession of faith so clear and so compelling that a significant number of those present were converted on the spot. Her witness before and after this act — she endured multiple tortures before her final death, and during each one she was observed praying with a tranquility that the sources describe as the most powerful apologetic of her martyrdom — is the tradition's model of how the body becomes, in the moment of its destruction, the most persuasive argument for the faith it bears.

Saint Alexander of Tiberias is the great discovery of this day and one of the most humanly touching figures in the synaxarium: a man who sold charcoal in the city of Tiberias in Palestine, who was so poor that he had nothing to offer the passing strangers and pilgrims he encountered except company and the occasional small warmth of his fire, but who gave even this with a generosity so habitual and so unself-conscious that the tradition preserves the testimony of multiple pilgrims who recalled his fire as the most memorable thing they had encountered on their journey. Alexander had no theological education, no monastic formation, no episcopal authority. He was a charcoal-seller who kept his fire burning and welcomed strangers. The tradition places him alongside the great Arsenius — the most educated man in the desert — to make its characteristic point: that the ascent to God is not bounded by class or education, that the charcoal-seller's fire is as genuine an icon of the divine warmth as the scholar's silence, and that the hospitality of the poor is a form of theology that the learned cannot teach but can only receive as gift. Amen.
